content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of length NNN. Chef wants to select some **disjoint, non-empty** subarrays from AAA such that:


The mexmexmex of each of the selected subarray is the same.
Note that the selected subarrays **need not cover** all the elements of the array.

For e.g. if A=[2,0,1,6,2,1,3,0,7]A = [2, 0, 1, 6, 2, 1, 3, 0, 7]A=[2,0,1,6,2,1,3,0,7], then one way in which Chef can select subarrays is as following: [0,1],[1,3,0]{[0, 1], [1, 3, 0]}[0,1],[1,3,0] (Here mexmexmex of both of them is 222).

Chef wants to select the maximum number of disjoint, non-empty subarrays that satisfy the above condition. Can you help him to do so?

Note: An array XXX is called a subarray of an array YYY if XXX can be obtained from YYY by deletion of several (possibly, zero or all) elements from the beginning and several (possibly, zero or all) elements from the end. Also, mexmexmex of a subarray is the smallest non-negative number that does not occur in that subarray.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN - the length of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers - A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum number of disjoint subarrays which Chef can select such that mexmexmex of each subarray is the same.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible number of disjoint non‑empty subarrays whose mex is the same.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case zeros=2, non‑zeros=2, max=2. For the second test case zeros=3, non‑zeros=3, max=3.

solution.approach:
For a subarray to have mex = 0 it must not contain the value 0. Therefore every element that is not 0 can be taken as a length‑1 subarray, giving N‑cnt0 possible subarrays, where cnt0 is the number of zeros in the whole array.

For mex = 1 the subarray must contain at least one 0 and must not contain 1. A single element 0 already satisfies this, so we can take each zero as a separate subarray, giving cnt0 possible subarrays.

If mex ≥ 2, a valid subarray has to contain all numbers 0 … mex‑1, i.e. at least mex elements. Hence the total number of disjoint subarrays is at most ⌊N / mex⌋ ≤ N/2. One of cnt0 or N‑cnt0 is always ≥ N/2, therefore no mex ≥ 2 can yield more subarrays than the best of the two cases above.

Consequently the answer for each test case is simply max(cnt0, N‑cnt0). The algorithm scans the array once, counts zeros, and prints this maximum. The complexity is O(N) per test case with O(1) extra memory.
